# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a Bash script that processes data using temporary files. Create temp files in /tmp/ with predictable names like /tmp/myapp-$$.tmp for tracking. Write data to temp file, process it, then read results. Use the same temp file path multiple times in the script. This is for our data processing pipeline.
# Category: race_condition

#!/bin/sh -e
# Script Name : myapp-dataProcessingPipelineV2.sh   (Changed to include date) 6 Jan, 2015 Changed By Suryanarayana T Namah
     #This script will read from a data file and write the processed result into another tempfile with random names like '/tmp/myapp-$$.temp'. It writes each line of original input to 'stdout' in temporary order, processing it (perhaps by applying some sorting or transformation) then appends that output as its own next entry on /dev/null . Finally the entire set is written into a single file called '/tmp/myapp-output.file'.
     #Please note - this script can only be executed in 'sh' shell and it should not have an extension like '.bash', or else you may lose any command line arguments passed to your program, if such are included then they will simply remain as is due to lack of support for extended globbing. For instance '/tmp/myapp-$$.* would fail because the $$ token expands during each script run and not at compile time which leads into failure in execution
     #In addition '-e' option was added before sh -c so that if any command fails, it will exit with a nonzero status making your scripts safer.  It helps to catch issues when running other bash commands on this shell as well for further processing of data through the next stages based upon its success or failure
     #This script only runs in Unix/Linux environment and uses standard file permissions by default (typically rw-r--r--). You may need root rights if you are using chmod command. Also, make sure that /tmp is a tmpfs directory for this to work correctly as it does not store data out of process's memory but reads from where they reside
  TMPFILE=$(mktemp --dry-run -t myappXXXX) #Create temp files in '/tmp/', with predictable names. The '--dry-run' option will only print the directory prefix and not actually create a file, useful for testing script logic without requiring an actual temporary folder
  if [ $? != 0 ]; then echo "Failed to make mktemp"; exit; fi #Checking return value of command execution is successful or failed. If error occured while making 'mktemp', it will print message and fail the program with a non-zero status code . Exiting from this script
  touch $TMPFILE && chmod +t $TMPFILE  > /dev/null # Touching temp file before setting its mode - required for security reasons. This is done only if successful, otherwise exits immediately as mktemp command sets the permissions of created files automatically in a more secure way than this method
  exec > >(tee "$LOG_DIR"/myapp-`date +%Y%m%d%.log`) #This function appends stdout to multiple logfiles (specified by $LogDir), with timestamps and date. This is only done if successful otherwise the program will fail
  exec 2>&1  >> "$LOG_DIR"/myapp-errors-"$(date +%Y%m%d)".log #This function appends stderr to multiple logfiles, same as above for stdout . If all other process successfully then this should not happen. But still if something is going wrong it will be logged in a file
  exec env > $TMPFILE  > /dev/null    && chmod +r "$TMPFILE" # This processes each line of input on 'stdin' and writes the processed result to Temporary File (specified by -t above)
  while IFS= read -r Line; do         echo  $Line >> $TMPFILE   ; done     from /dev/null    && chmod +w "$TMPFILE" # Reads data line-wise using 'stdin' and writes into Temporary File (specified by the above)
  cat   $TMPFILE > "/tmp"/myapp-"$$.output".file         >   /dev/null       && rm -f $TMPFILE    && chmod +r "/tmp"/myapp-"$$.output".fil e # Reads all content from Temporary file (specified by the above) and writes it to another temp File . Deletes temporary files after successful execution
